title: Generative single-cell transcriptomics via large language models
abstract:
ABSTRACT

Single-cell and spatial transcriptomics have generated vast atlases of cellular states, yet these data are almost exclusively used for analysis rather than generation. Here we introduce the LLM-based model PGL, Portraying Gene Language, a framework that reframes single-cell transcriptomes as a language generation modeling problem. PGL represents each cell as a long sequence of gene–expression tokens and uses a large language model to synthesize complete single-cell RNA-seq profiles from metadata alone, such as tissue and disease context. PGL-generated cells recapitulate dataset-specific transcriptomic structure, align with known cancer subtype biology, and mix coherently with real single-cell datasets. Notably, generated cells can be used as effective references for spatial transcriptomics, enabling accurate cell-type mapping without matched single-cell atlases. By shifting single-cell modeling from representation learning to cell generation, PGL enables virtual cohort construction, hypothesis generation, and reference-on-demand analysis, positioning generative language models as foundational tools for in silico single-cell biology.
ai_for_science_reason: The paper uses a large language model to generate single-cell RNA-seq profiles, contributing to scientific discovery in biology and medicine.
implementation_justification: The paper presents a novel application of LLMs in generating single-cell data.
review_justification: The abstract provides a clear overview of the methodology and its scientific implications.
benchmark_justification: The paper mentions alignment with known biology, suggesting some benchmarking.
hypothesis_generation_justification: The model enables virtual cohort construction and hypothesis generation.
scientific_discovery_justification: The generative approach aids in scientific discovery by creating new data for analysis.
experiment_design_justification: The paper implies potential for experiment design through virtual cohort construction.
material_discovery_justification: The focus is on biological data, not material discovery.
molecular_design_justification: The paper does not address molecular design.
drug_discovery_justification: The paper does not specifically target drug discovery.
causal_discovery_justification: The generative model may aid in understanding causal relationships in transcriptomics.
biomedical_discovery_justification: The application of LLMs to single-cell data directly supports biomedical discovery.
idea_development_justification: The paper introduces a novel concept of using LLMs for single-cell data generation.
paper_title: Generative single-cell transcriptomics via large language models

objective.answer: The primary objective of the paper is to introduce the PGL (Portraying Gene Language) framework, which reframes single-cell transcriptomes as a language generation modeling problem. The authors aim to synthesize complete single-cell RNA-seq profiles from metadata alone, such as tissue and disease context, using a large language model.
objective.evidence: Here we introduce the LLM-based model PGL, Portraying Gene Language, a framework that reframes single-cell transcriptomes as a language generation modeling problem. PGL represents each cell as a long sequence of gene–expression tokens and uses a large language model to synthesize complete single-cell RNA-seq profiles from metadata alone, such as tissue and disease context.

knowledge_gap.answer: The knowledge gap addressed by the paper is the lack of exploration in generating new, biologically plausible single cells in silico, as existing models primarily treat single-cell data as an embedding or representation learning problem.
knowledge_gap.evidence: Despite these advances, existing transformer and language-based models primarily treat single-cell data as an embedding or representation learning problem. Their outputs are typically low-dimensional vectors optimized for prediction or classification tasks. As a result, the notion of cell generation—producing a new single cell in silico with thousands of genes expressed in a coherent and condition-consistent manner—has remained largely unexplored.

answer: The PGL framework represents single-cell transcriptomes as a language generation problem, allowing the use of large language models to generate complete single-cell RNA-seq profiles.
PGL-generated cells can be used as effective references for spatial transcriptomics, enabling accurate cell-type mapping without matched single-cell atlases.
The framework shifts single-cell modeling from representation learning to cell generation, enabling virtual cohort construction and hypothesis generation.
evidence: Here we introduce the LLM-based model PGL, Portraying Gene Language, a framework that reframes single-cell transcriptomes as a language generation modeling problem.
Notably, generated cells can be used as effective references for spatial transcriptomics, enabling accurate cell-type mapping without matched single-cell atlases.
By shifting single-cell modeling from representation learning to cell generation, PGL enables virtual cohort construction, hypothesis generation, and reference-on-demand analysis.

inspirational_papers.answer:
- Park et al. (2024) CELLama: foundation model for single cell and spatial transcriptomics by cell embedding leveraging language model abilities. (Methodological precursors)
- Levine et al. (2024) Cell2Sentence: teaching large language models the language of biology. (Methodological precursors)
inspirational_papers.evidence: In parallel, language model–driven approaches such as CELLama and C2S have explored sequence-based formulations of single-cell and spatial transcriptomics, reframing gene expression profiles as languages to embed cellular features.

steps.input: Single-cell RNA-seq data from the scBaseCount repository, focusing on cancer datasets.
steps.output: A training corpus of approximately 14 million cells derived from 2,118 independent datasets.
steps.evidence: Single-cell RNA-seq data were curated from the scBaseCount repository, focusing on cancer datasets profiled using droplet-based platforms.

steps.output: Discretized gene expression values into five ordinal categories: Absent, Low, Medium, High, and Very High.
steps.evidence: Continuous gene expression values were discretized into five ordinal categories: Absent, Low, Medium, High, and Very High.

steps.input: Gene-expression token sequences conditioned on metadata.
steps.output: A trained PGL model capable of generating gene-expression sequences.
steps.evidence: The model was trained using a causal language modeling objective to maximize the likelihood of the gene-expression token sequence conditioned on metadata and preceding tokens.

steps.output: Generated gene-expression sequences parsed into a gene-by-cell expression matrix.
steps.evidence: At inference, the model receives only cell-level metadata as input and autoregressively generates a gene-expression sequence between <START_GEN> and <END_GEN> tokens.

tools.name: Qwen-0.6B
tools.description: Used as the base decoder-only transformer for training the PGL model.
tools.evidence: After training the PGL model on a general-purpose text-based language model (Qwen-0.6B), the model generated sentences in an autoregressive manner.

tools.name: TACCO
tools.description: Used for spatial cell type mapping using an optimal transport–based deconvolution method.
tools.evidence: These annotated cell types were then transferred to prostate cancer Visium data using an optimal transport–based deconvolution method (TACCO).

benchmark_datasets.name: scBaseCount
benchmark_datasets.usage: Used for training and evaluation of the PGL model.
benchmark_datasets.evidence: Single-cell RNA-seq data were curated from the scBaseCount repository.

evaluation_metrics.name: Spearman rank correlation
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures global transcriptomic similarity between generated and real datasets.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to compare gene expression distributions of generated and real datasets.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: We first evaluated global transcriptomic similarity by computing Spearman rank correlations between gene expression distributions of generated and real datasets.

evaluation_metrics.name: Silhouette score
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Quantifies mixing and separability between generated and real cells.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to assess single-cell level integration and mixing.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: To assess transcriptomic similarity at the single-cell level, we integrated LLM-generated and real scRNA-seq datasets and quantified their degree of mixing using silhouette scores.

evaluation_metrics.name: Jaccard similarity
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures overlap in transcriptomic marker features between generated and real datasets.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to quantify marker gene overlap.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: We further quantified overlap in transcriptomic marker features between generated and real scRNA-seq data using Jaccard similarity metrics.

methods.name: Hypothesis or Idea Generation
methods.description: The system produces candidate hypotheses or new research ideas from prior knowledge or external input.
methods.evidence: By shifting single-cell modeling from representation learning to cell generation, PGL enables virtual cohort construction, hypothesis generation, and reference-on-demand analysis.

methods.name: Experimental design generation
methods.description: The approach includes producing experimental protocols, configurations, or evaluation strategies.
methods.evidence: We demonstrate that virtually generated single cell data recapitulate key statistical and biological properties of real data.

areas.name: Biological Sciences
areas.description: The paper develops a generative framework for single-cell transcriptomics.
areas.evidence: PGL enables virtual cohort construction, hypothesis generation, and reference-on-demand analysis, positioning generative language models as foundational tools for in silico single-cell biology.

areas.name: Health Sciences
areas.description: The framework is applied to generate single-cell RNA-seq profiles for cancer research.
areas.evidence: Single-cell RNA-seq data were curated from the scBaseCount repository, focusing on cancer datasets profiled using droplet-based platforms.

performance_summary.summary: PGL-generated cells exhibit global properties comparable to real scRNA-seq datasets, with higher Spearman correlations and lower silhouette scores for matched metadata.
performance_summary.evidence: The mean Spearman correlation was significantly higher for same metadata datasets than for different metadata controls (0.47±0.04 vs. 0.34±0.05; p < 0.001), indicating that generated scRNA-seq data more closely resemble real scRNA-seq data derived from the same biological context.

baselines.name: Real scRNA-seq datasets
baselines.description: Used as a baseline for comparison with generated datasets.
baselines.evidence: We evaluated whether LLM-generated scRNA-seq data exhibit global properties comparable to those of real scRNA-seq datasets.

benchmark_datasets.name: scBaseCount
benchmark_datasets.usage: Used for training and evaluation of the PGL model.
benchmark_datasets.evidence: Single-cell RNA-seq data were curated from the scBaseCount repository.

evaluation_metrics.name: Spearman rank correlation
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures global transcriptomic similarity between generated and real datasets.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to compare gene expression distributions of generated and real datasets.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: We first evaluated global transcriptomic similarity by computing Spearman rank correlations between gene expression distributions of generated and real datasets.

evaluation_metrics.purpose: Quantifies mixing and separability between generated and real cells.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to assess single-cell level integration and mixing.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: To assess transcriptomic similarity at the single-cell level, we integrated LLM-generated and real scRNA-seq datasets and quantified their degree of mixing using silhouette scores.

evaluation_metrics.name: Jaccard similarity
evaluation_metrics.purpose: Measures overlap in transcriptomic marker features between generated and real datasets.
evaluation_metrics.application: Used to quantify marker gene overlap.
evaluation_metrics.evidence: We further quantified overlap in transcriptomic marker features between generated and real scRNA-seq data using Jaccard similarity metrics.

benchmark_dataset.name: scBaseCount
benchmark_dataset.usage: Used for training and evaluation of the PGL model.
benchmark_dataset.evidence: Single-cell RNA-seq data were curated from the scBaseCount repository.

limitations.name: Expression Discretization
limitations.description: Discretizing gene expression into a small number of ordinal categories compresses the continuous dynamic range of raw count data.
limitations.evidence: First, discretizing gene expression into a small number of ordinal categories facilitates the formulation of transcriptomes as a language modeling problem, but inevitably compresses the continuous dynamic range of raw count data.

limitations.name: Generation Capacity
limitations.description: The generation capacity of PGL is constrained by the maximum context length of current LLMs.
limitations.evidence: Second, the generation capacity of PGL is constrained by the maximum context length of current LLMs.

future_directions.name: Incorporate finer-grained discretization schemes
future_directions.description: Future extensions could incorporate finer-grained discretization schemes or hybrid formulations that combine categorical tokens with continuous-valued outputs.
future_directions.evidence: Future extensions could incorporate finer-grained discretization schemes or hybrid formulations that combine categorical tokens with continuous-valued outputs.

future_directions.name: Expand effective gene coverage
future_directions.description: Iterative or multi-pass generation strategies may further expand effective gene coverage without exceeding context limits.
future_directions.evidence: Additionally, iterative or multi-pass generation strategies may further expand effective gene coverage without exceeding context limits.
